content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacrumcnto, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kenneth R. Hetge 
Tehachapi City Councilmember 
333 S. Dennison Road, #65 
Tehachapi, CA 93561 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-078 
Dear Mr. Hetge: 
May 4, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act") 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any 
other area oflaw, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice 
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
As required by Section 1097.1 (c)(3), we have forwarded your request to the Attorney 
General's Office and the Kem County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. (See Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for 
purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against 
any individual other than the requester." (Section I 097. l(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hetge from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the City's potential termination of an existing ground lease (the "nine-hanger ground 
lease") for Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("TMA") lands and entrance into individual ground leases 
of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease with the subtenants of those 
lands? 
2. Does Section I 090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the 
making of, or the City from entering into, potential individual ground leases of the TMA lands 
currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease with the subtenants of those lands? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-17-078 
Page No. 2 
1. No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating 
to the potential termination of the nine-hangar ground lease and the City's entrance into individual 
ground leases with subtenants of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease 
because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Councilmember's financial interests. 
2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in 
the making of, or the City from entering into, the potential individual ground leases of the TMA 
lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease because the Councilmember is not 
financially interested in those leases. 
FACTS 
The City of Tehachapi owns the Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("TMA"), and the TMA is 
wholly located within the City limits. The TMA is situated on 264 acres, and is home to 95 aircraft 
and 80 hangars. The City offers three different types ofleases ofTMA lands, and charges a specific 
rental rate for each type ofTMA lands available for lease. The current rental rates for each type of 
lease are as follows: hangar leases (including a hangar and the underlying TMA lands) at $0.255 per 
square foot; ground leases (including only the underlying TMA lands) at $0.055 per square foot; 
and unimproved land at $0.15 per square foot. The rental rate for each City lease of TMA lands is 
set upon the parties' entrance into the lease, and is fixed for the life of the lease subject only to Cost 
Price Index increases. 
In 2005, the City entered into a ground lease with a tenant for TMA lands (the ••nine-hangar 
ground lease"). The tenant then constructed nine hangars on those lands, subleased the underlying 
TMA lands, and sold the hangars to the subtenants of the underlying TMA lands. Last year, the 
tenant assigned the nine-hangar ground lease to one of those subtenants, and the new tenant has 
recently requested that the City terminate the nine-hangar ground lease and enter into new, 
individual ground leases with the subtenants of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar 
ground lease. The rent under the individual ground leases would be set at the current rate of $0.055 
per square foot upon the parties' entrance into the leases. This rental rate would be a slight increase 
over the rent the subtenants pay under their current subleases. 
For over ten years, Councilmember Helge and his wife have owned and operated an aviation 
business located on TMA lands. The Councilmember and his wife are the only employees of the 
business and each assists in the business's management, and the Councilmember and his wife have 
invested substantially more than $2,000 in the business. The Councilmember's business leases 
hangars, rents aircraft, and provides aircraft-maintenance services. The business owns six hangars 
located on TMA lands, and it leases the TMA lands underlying those hangars pursuant to long-term 
ground leases with the City. The Councilmember's hangars and long-term leases of TMA lands 
from the City are separate and distinct from the nine-hangar ground lease at issue. 
The Councilmember's business's long-term ground leases will not expire during the 
Councilmember's current four-year term of office or during his potential next four-year term of 


File No. A-17-078 
Page No. 3 
office. The Councilmember' s business subleases four of its six hangars and the TMA lands 
underlying them to tenants, and its aircraft-rental and aircraft-maintenance operations are conducted 
out of the remaining two hangars. All four of the Councilmember's business's hangars available for 
rent are subleased and occupied, and the subtenants have indicated to the Councilmember that they 
intend to remain subtenants of his business for the foreseeable future. 
The Councilmember and City officers and employees provided additional facts in 
subsequent correspondence. On April 26, 2017, the City's Administrative Advisor/Deputy City 
Clerk advised that the rental rate applicable to the potential individual ground leases will be set 
according to the City's schedule of rental rates set forth above upon the parties entering into each 
lease, and that those potential leases' rental rate will have no bearing on the rental rate of the 
Councilmember's long-term ground leases with the City. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests 
identified in that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the Councilmember's interests 
implicated by the facts presented and the decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground lease or the 
potential individual ground leases are the following: 
• An interest in any business entity in which the official has invested $2,000 or more, or in which 
the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds a position of management. 
(Section 87103( a) and ( d).) The Councilmember has an interest in his aviation business because 
he and his wife have invested greater than $2,000 in it, and because he and his wife jointly 
manage its operations. 
• An interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) The Councilmember has an interest in his long-term leases 
ofTMA lands with the City ifhe has invested $2,000 or more in those leaseholds. 
• An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The 
Councilmember has interests in sources of income to his business, including its hangar tenants, 
aircraft-rental customers, and aircraft-maintenance customers. 
• An interest in the official's personal finances or those of an immediate family member. (See 
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 


File No. A-17-078 
Page No. 4 
Therefore, the Councilmember has interests in his aviation business, long-term ground 
leaseholds ofTMA lands, and sources of income to his business, as well as an interest in his own 
personal finances and those of immediate family members. 2 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to 
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision; and an 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. If 
the official's interest is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 1870l(b) provides that 
the decision's effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
The Councilmember's interests in his business, real property, sources of income, and his 
personal finances and those of immediate family members are not explicitly involved in the 
decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground lease and the potential individual ground leases. 
Therefore, those decisions' effect on the Councilmember's respective interests is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
The Councilmember's Business 
Regulation 18702.I(b) provides that a decision's effect on a business interest is material if a 
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect 
would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business at issue. That regulation 
also provides examples of types of decisions that would contribute to such a change in value, 
including decisions that would increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which 
the business entity is engaged. (See Regulation 18702.l(b)(2).) 
The Councilmember's business's hangars and long-term leases ofTMA lands from the City 
are separate and distinct from the nine-hangar ground lease at issue. Those long-term leases of 
TMA lands will not expire during the Councilmember's current four-year term of office or his next 
potential four-year term of office. The rental rate for each City lease ofTMA lands, including the 
Councilmember's leases, is set upon the parties' entrance into the lease and is fixed for the life of 
the lease subject only to Cost Price Index increases. And the City's Administrative Advisor/Deputy 
City Clerk has confirmed that the rental rate to be applied to the potential individual ground leases 
with the City will have no bearing on the rental rate of the Councilmember's long-term ground 
leases. 
2 Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and 
subdivision ( c) of that regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in his or her business or 
real property, materiality is detennined pursuant to Regulation l 8702.1, relating to business interests, or Regulation 
18702.2, relating to real property interests, respectively. Because the decisions at issue affect the Councilmember's 
interests in his business and real property, we apply the materiality standards applicable to those interests, and we do 
not further address the Councilmember's interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family members. 


File No. A-17-078 
Page No. 5 
Furthermore, there is no indication that the decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground 
lease would increase or decrease the amount of competition for the Councilmember's business's 
services. All four of the Councilmember's business's hangars available for rent are subleased and 
occupied, and the subtenants have indicated to the Councilmember that they intend to remain 
subtenants of his business for the foreseeable future. Finally, there is also no indication that the 
decisions at issue would displace any of the current subtenants of the TMA lands subject to the 
nine-hangar lease. 
The decisions relating to the nine-hangar lease and the potential individual ground leases 
would not result in an increase or decrease of the amount of competition for the Councilmember's 
business's services. And there is no indication of any other effect of the decisions at issue on the 
Councilmember's business. Therefore, we find that the decisions at issue would not contribute to a 
change in the value of the Councilmember's business. 
The Councilmember's Leaseholds 
A decision's effect on any real property in which the official has a leasehold interest is 
material if the decision will: (I) change the termination date of the lease; (2) increase or decrease 
the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental value of the property, 
and the official has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the official's actual or legally 
allowable use of the real property; or (5) impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real 
property. (Regulation 18702.2(b).) 
The decisions at issue will not change the termination date of the Councilmember's long­
term leases ofTMA lands; increase or decrease the actual or potential rental value of his leased 
TMA lands; change his actual or legally allowable use of the real property; or impact his use and 
enjoyment of those TMA lands. Therefore, we find that the decisions would not have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's long-term leases ofTMA lands. 
The Councilmember's Sources of Income 
With respect to income received by an official for services provided in the ordinary course 
of business, Regulation l 8702.3(a) provides the applicable materiality standards. These standards 
vary depending on whether the source is explicitly or inexplicitly involved, and whether the source 
is an individual, business, or nonprofit. 
The Councilmember's business subleases four of its six hangars to subtenants, and its 
aircraft-rental and aircraft maintenance operations are conducted out of its remaining two hangars. 
According to the Councilmember, the decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground lease or the 
potential individual ground leases would not have a financial effect on any of his sources of income 
to the best of his knowledge. And the facts presented do not indicate that the decisions at issue 
would affect any of the Councilmember's sources of income in any way. Therefore, we find that the 
decisions at issue would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Councilmember's sources of income based on the facts presented. 


File No. A-17-078 
Page No. 6 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking 
part in decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground lease or the potential individual ground leases 
because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or 
more of his interests. 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 prohibits a public official from being financially interested in a contract in both the 
official's public and private capacities. (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47Cal.4th 1050, 1073.) A 
contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair 
and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 
is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be 
avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded 
from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: (I) does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or 
noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply? 
Steps one through three are not at issue here. A public officer is involved because a city 
councilmember is a public officer. Contracts are involved because the City is considering 
terminating the nine-hangar ground lease and entering into individual ground leases with the 
subtenants of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease. Because an officer 
is conclusively presumed to participate in the making of his or her agency's contracts when the 
officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the contract at issue 
(Thomson, supra, p. 649), the Councilmember will be presumed to have participated in the making 
of those individual leases. 
Step Four: Does the Councilmember have a financial interest in the potential individual 
ground leases of TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease? 
For Section 1090 to apply, the official in question must have a financial interest in the 
contract at issue. "The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is 
diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he 
acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 ( citations omitted).) 
Neither the Councilmember nor his business are a party to the nine-hanger ground lease. 
The Councilmember's own long-term leases of TMA lands with the City will not expire during his 
current four-year term of office or his potential next four-year term of office, the rental rates for 
those leases are fixed subject only to Cost Price Index increases, and the City's Administrative 
Advisor/Deputy City Clerk has confirmed that the potential individual ground leases' rental rate 
will have no bearing on the rental rate for the Councilmember's leases. 
The potential individual ground leases would not create additional competition for the 
Councilmember's business's services, affect the terms or conditions of his long-term leases of TMA 


File No. A-17-078 
Page No. 7 
lands, or affect any of his sources of income, based on the facts presented. Therefore, we find that 
the Councilmember is not financially interested in the potential individual ground leases at issue for 
purposes of Section 1090. 
Accordingly, we conclude that Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from 
making or participating in, or the City from entering into, the potential individual ground leases, 
and this Section-I 090 analysis ends here. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matt ew . 
s y 
Counsel, Legal Division

currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease with the subtenants of those lands?

sections.analysis:
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests

potential individual ground leases are the following:

the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds a position of management. 
(Section 87103( a) and ( d).) The Councilmember has an interest in his aviation business because 
he and his wife have invested greater than $2,000 in it, and because he and his wife jointly 
manage its operations. 
• An interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) The Councilmember has an interest in his long-term leases 
ofTMA lands with the City ifhe has invested $2,000 or more in those leaseholds.

promised to the official within 12 months of the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The 
Councilmember has interests in sources of income to his business, including its hangar tenants,

Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.  
Therefore, the Councilmember has interests in his aviation business, long-term ground 
leaseholds ofTMA lands, and sources of income to his business, as well as an interest in his own 
personal finances and those of immediate family members. 2 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to 
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision; and an 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,

the official's interest is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 1870l(b) provides that 
the decision's effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
The Councilmember's interests in his business, real property, sources of income, and his 
personal finances and those of immediate family members are not explicitly involved in the 
decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground lease and the potential individual ground leases. 
Therefore, those decisions' effect on the Councilmember's respective interests is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
The Councilmember's Business 
Regulation 18702.I(b) provides that a decision's effect on a business interest is material if a 
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect 
would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business at issue. That regulation

including decisions that would increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which 
the business entity is engaged. (See Regulation 18702.l(b)(2).) 
The Councilmember's business's hangars and long-term leases ofTMA lands from the City 
are separate and distinct from the nine-hangar ground lease at issue. Those long-term leases of 
TMA lands will not expire during the Councilmember's current four-year term of office or his next 
potential four-year term of office. The rental rate for each City lease ofTMA lands, including the 
Councilmember's leases, is set upon the parties' entrance into the lease and is fixed for the life of 
the lease subject only to Cost Price Index increases. And the City's Administrative Advisor/Deputy

with the City will have no bearing on the rental rate of the Councilmember's long-term ground 
leases. 
2 Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and 
subdivision ( c) of that regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in his or her business or 
real property, materiality is detennined pursuant to Regulation l 8702.1, relating to business interests, or Regulation 
18702.2, relating to real property interests, respectively. Because the decisions at issue affect the Councilmember's 
interests in his business and real property, we apply the materiality standards applicable to those interests, and we do 
not further address the Councilmember's interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family members.  
Furthermore, there is no indication that the decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground 
lease would increase or decrease the amount of competition for the Councilmember's business's 
services. All four of the Councilmember's business's hangars available for rent are subleased and 
occupied, and the subtenants have indicated to the Councilmember that they intend to remain

nine-hangar lease. 
The decisions relating to the nine-hangar lease and the potential individual ground leases 
would not result in an increase or decrease of the amount of competition for the Councilmember's 
business's services. And there is no indication of any other effect of the decisions at issue on the

The Councilmember's Leaseholds 
A decision's effect on any real property in which the official has a leasehold interest is 
material if the decision will: (I) change the termination date of the lease; (2) increase or decrease 
the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental value of the property, 
and the official has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the official's actual or legally 
allowable use of the real property; or (5) impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real 
property. (Regulation 18702.2(b).) 
The decisions at issue will not change the termination date of the Councilmember's long­
term leases ofTMA lands; increase or decrease the actual or potential rental value of his leased 
TMA lands; change his actual or legally allowable use of the real property; or impact his use and 
enjoyment of those TMA lands. Therefore, we find that the decisions would not have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's long-term leases ofTMA lands. 
The Councilmember's Sources of Income 
With respect to income received by an official for services provided in the ordinary course 
of business, Regulation l 8702.3(a) provides the applicable materiality standards. These standards 
vary depending on whether the source is explicitly or inexplicitly involved, and whether the source 
is an individual, business, or nonprofit. 
The Councilmember's business subleases four of its six hangars to subtenants, and its 
aircraft-rental and aircraft maintenance operations are conducted out of its remaining two hangars. 
According to the Councilmember, the decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground lease or the

decisions at issue would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Councilmember's sources of income based on the facts presented.  
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking 
part in decisions relating to the nine-hangar ground lease or the potential individual ground leases 
because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or 
more of his interests. 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 prohibits a public official from being financially interested in a contract in both the 
official's public and private capacities. (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47Cal.4th 1050, 1073.) A 
contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair 
and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 
is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be 
avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded 
from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: (I) does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or 
noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply? 
Steps one through three are not at issue here. A public officer is involved because a city 
councilmember is a public officer. Contracts are involved because the City is considering 
terminating the nine-hangar ground lease and entering into individual ground leases with the 
subtenants of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease. Because an officer 
is conclusively presumed to participate in the making of his or her agency's contracts when the 
officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the contract at issue 
(Thomson, supra, p. 649), the Councilmember will be presumed to have participated in the making 
of those individual leases. 
Step Four: Does the Councilmember have a financial interest in the potential individual 
ground leases of TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease? 
For Section 1090 to apply, the official in question must have a financial interest in the 
contract at issue. "The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is 
diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he 
acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 ( citations omitted).) 
Neither the Councilmember nor his business are a party to the nine-hanger ground lease. 
The Councilmember's own long-term leases of TMA lands with the City will not expire during his 
current four-year term of office or his potential next four-year term of office, the rental rates for 
those leases are fixed subject only to Cost Price Index increases, and the City's Administrative 
Advisor/Deputy City Clerk has confirmed that the potential individual ground leases' rental rate 
will have no bearing on the rental rate for the Councilmember's leases. 
The potential individual ground leases would not create additional competition for the 
Councilmember's business's services, affect the terms or conditions of his long-term leases of TMA  
lands, or affect any of his sources of income, based on the facts presented. Therefore, we find that 
the Councilmember is not financially interested in the potential individual ground leases at issue for 
purposes of Section 1090. 
Accordingly, we conclude that Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from 
making or participating in, or the City from entering into, the potential individual ground leases, 
and this Section-I 090 analysis ends here.

QUESTION: 1. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hetge from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the City's potential termination of an existing ground lease (the "nine-hanger ground 
lease") for Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("TMA") lands and entrance into individual ground leases 
of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease with the subtenants of those 
lands? 
2. Does Section I 090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the 
making of, or the City from entering into, potential individual ground leases of the TMA lands 
currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease with the subtenants of those lands? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in

CONCLUSION: 1. No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating 
to the potential termination of the nine-hangar ground lease and the City's entrance into individual 
ground leases with subtenants of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease 
because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Councilmember's financial interests. 
2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in 
the making of, or the City from entering into, the potential individual ground leases of the TMA 
lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease because the Councilmember is not 
financially interested in those leases.

Dennison Road, #65 Tehachapi, CA 93561 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-078 Dear Mr. Hetge: May 4, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area oflaw, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. As required by Section 1097.1 (c)(3), we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kem County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (See Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requester." (Section I 097. l(c)(5).) QUESTIONS 1. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hetge from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the City's potential termination of an existing ground lease (the "nine-hanger ground lease") for Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("TMA") lands and entrance into individual ground leases of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease with the subtenants of those lands? 2. Does Section I 090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, potential individual ground leases of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease with the subtenants of those lands? 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSIONS File No. A-17-078 Page No. 2 1. No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the potential termination of the nine-hangar ground lease and the City's entrance into individual ground leases with subtenants of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial interests. 2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, the potential individual ground leases of the TMA lands currently subject to the nine-hangar ground lease because the Councilmember is not financially interested in those leases.
